source_file: A Spore-Forming Probiotic Supplement Improves the Intestinal Immune Response and Protects the Intestinal Health During Recurrent Clostridioides difficile Colonization in Mice.txt

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation mitigates antibiotic-induced changes in cecum weight.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Probiotic-supplemented mice had significantly higher cecum weight compared to CD-S mice (P < .001).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation increases mRNA expression of immune parameters and reduces serum iron levels.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Probiotic-supplemented mice had increased messenger RNA expression of several immune parameters, accompanied by lower serum iron levels compared with CD-S mice (P < .05).

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation reduces intestinal inflammation by lowering TNFα and calprotectin expression.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: Lower expressions of TNFα and calprotectin (P ≤ .05) were observed in CD-PRO mice compared with CD-S.

biological_mechanisms.mechanism: Probiotic supplementation supports intestinal tight junction protein expression.
biological_mechanisms.evidence: The probiotics also supported the expression of intestinal tight junction proteins, which were diminished in the proximal colon of CD-S mice (P < .05).

key_findings: Mice supplemented with targeted spore-forming probiotics exhibited improved immune responses and nutrition immunity properties during recurrent CD colonization.
Probiotic supplementation was linked with less inflammation and enhanced intestinal barrier proteins.
Probiotic-supplemented mice had lower serum iron levels compared to CD-S mice.
relevance_to_research_topic: This paper demonstrates that modulation of the gut environment (via probiotics) can alter serum iron levels and influence the host immune response and intestinal barrier function during C. difficile colonization. Although the paper does not study high-iron diet directly, it highlights the role of iron (serum iron) as a component of 'nutrition immunity' in CDI, which is relevant to understanding how dietary iron might affect CDI susceptibility and severity through interactions with the gut microbiota and host immune system.
